content:
Suno AI Sound Design Series — Part 2 | B2B Music Series — Part 1

8 min read
Just now

--

Coffee shop jazz is one of the most-searched music moods on YouTube. Entire channels built on nothing but looping playlists have millions of subscribers.
The listener demand is real. What most AI music creators miss is that the demand exists on the supply side too, every independent café running Spotify is paying a performing rights license they’d rather not pay.
This article is the same structure as Part 1 of this series, but applied to a tighter sub-niche: coffee shop jazz, specifically built for commercial B2B sale to café and independent coffee shop owners. Prompt system first, pitch playbook second.
Coffee shop jazz breaks into four time-slot sub-styles morning slow, study lo-fi, afternoon swing, evening intimate. Each needs different prompt logic. This article gives you all four systems, the pack structure, and the exact café owner pitch: email, pricing, delivery, and rights.
Why Coffee Shop Jazz Is the Right B2B Niche
Three things make this sub-niche work better than generic background music:
The buyer is obvious and local. Every independent café near you is a potential client. You don’t need a platform. You need one short email and a sample track.
The emotional brief is narrow. Coffee shop jazz has to feel present enough to fill silence but absent enough not to compete with conversation. That tight constraint is easier to prompt for than a vague “relaxing jazz”
The licensing gap is real. Independent cafés using Spotify or Apple Music for commercial play are technically required to hold a performing rights license : SACEM, SOCAN, PPL, GEMA depending on country. Many small operators don’t. Original owned music sidesteps this entirely. That’s a cost argument with legs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Four Time-Slot Sub-Styles
A real café’s music needs change across the day. Morning regulars ordering their first coffee need something different from a laptop study crowd at noon or a quieter after-work crowd at 7pm.
Most AI music packs ignore this. One generic “coffee shop jazz” playlist for the full day. The time-slot system is what makes a pack feel professional enough for a client to pay for it.
Slot 1 : Morning Slow Jazz (6–10 AM)
Function: Ease the first customers in. Low stimulation. The room is waking up.
Listener state: Pre-caffeine, not ready for energy, needs quiet presence.
Slow jazz, solo piano and soft brushed snare, upright bass walking quietly,
60 BPM, early morning feel, unhurried and warm,
1950s small club recording aesthetic, analog tape warmth,
no loud accents, no saxophone lead, no drama,
notes breathing between each phrase,
gentle swing feel, quiet but not empty, 4 minutesThe phrase “notes breathing between each phrase” is doing precise work. It prompts Suno toward space rests, gaps, the silence between notes that makes slow jazz feel intentional rather than empty.
Generate 5–6 tracks for this slot. Reject any output where the piano does something that feels like a statement. Morning music has the lowest tolerance for energy spikes.
Slot 2 : Study Lo-Fi Jazz (10 AM–2 PM)
Function: Hold focus without demanding attention. Highest-traffic slot for most independent cafés laptops, notebooks, people working.
Listener state: Focused, needs background consistency, breaks concentration if the music changes dramatically.
Lo-fi jazz, upright bass and piano trio, brushed drums, muted trumpet accents,
85 BPM, medium swing, consistent energy throughout —
no dramatic builds or drops, no dynamic peaks,
warm tape hiss texture, slightly compressed sound,
vintage recording feel, Thelonious Monk adjacent but lighter,
background study music, 5 minutes“No dramatic builds or drops” is the critical instruction. Study music breaks focus the moment it changes energy. Flat dynamics is the goal counterintuitive for jazz, where dynamics are usually a virtue, but correct for this function.
“Tape hiss texture” activates a lo-fi warmth that makes AI jazz feel less clinical. Test it with and without, the difference is audible.
Generate 7–8 tracks here. This is the longest slot and needs the most variety to avoid repetition fatigue.
Slot 3 : Afternoon Swing (2–6 PM)
Function: Lift energy for the post-lunch crowd. More social, more movement. The café is louder — the music can rise with it.
Listener state: Alert, conversational, ambient energy is welcome.
Medium swing jazz, piano trio with ride cymbal prominent,
upright bass walking, 105 BPM, bright and social,
afternoon café energy, 1960s Blue Note recording style,
clear mix, warm room acoustics,
short melodic phrases, not bebop complexity,
approachable and light, café not concert hall, 4 minutes“Café not concert hall” keeps the arrangement accessible no extended solos, no harmonic complexity that makes the music demand attention. The ride cymbal instruction matters specifically here. Afternoon swing lives in the cymbal pattern more than anywhere else.
Slot 4 : Evening Intimate Jazz (6–10 PM)
Function: Signal the shift from daytime to evening. Conversations are closer, groups are smaller. The music should feel like it moved closer too.
Listener state: Relaxed, winding down, present in the room.
Intimate jazz duo, solo saxophone and piano, no drums, no bass,
75 BPM, minor key with warmth — not sad, introspective,
candlelight atmosphere, small room natural acoustics,
late evening café feel, Bill Evans adjacent piano softness,
long melodic phrases, deliberate space between phrases,
no vibrato excess, 4.5 minutesDropping the rhythm section for this slot is deliberate. Drums and bass carry daytime energy. A sax-piano duo creates physical intimacy, the listener feels closer to the music, which matches how conversation changes at night.
This format is the hardest to get right. Plan for more generations here than any other slot 6–8 minimum before selecting.
Two production steps before packaging:
Loudness normalization. Suno AI outputs vary in perceived volume. A café owner playing these back on a single-zone system will notice volume jumps immediately. Auphonic’s free tier covers 2 hours/month. For a full pack you’ll need either their basic paid tier ($11/month) or pay-per-use at roughly $0.89/hour.
File naming. Use a clear system: 01-morning-slow-jazz-01.wav, 09-study-lofi-jazz-01.wav.
Café owners are not technical. Organized files are a quality signal that makes the product feel professional before they play a single track.
The B2B Café Owner Pitch
Independent café owners are not searching Gumroad for music packs. You need to reach them directly.
Step 1 : Find the Right Cafés
Target independent cafés not chains. Chains have centralized music systems. Independent operators make their own decisions.
The filter: Google Maps + “coffee shop” or “café” in your city or any city you want to target. Look at the reviews. You’re scanning for:

• Reviews that mention “great atmosphere” or “love the music”, they care about sound

• No mention of live music, open mic, or a resident DJ, they’re using recorded music

• Fewer than 3 locations decision maker is probably the owner, not a manager
That profile is your prospect. They care about atmosphere, they’re using recorded music right now, and you can reach the decision maker directly.
Step 2 : The Qualifying Question
One thing tells you whether the pitch has legs: are they currently paying for a commercial music license?
If yes — your pitch is “owned music you never pay for again”
If no — your pitch is “music that’s legally yours, no annual fees, no compliance risk”
Both work. The framing is different.
Step 3 — The Pitch Email
Subject: Custom coffee shop music for [Café Name] — no licensing fees
Body:
Hi [Name],
I create original coffee shop jazz packs specifically designed for independent cafés morning slow, study hour, afternoon swing, evening intimate. Four moods, one full operating day.
The music is AI-generated, commercially cleared, and yours to keep. No Spotify license. No annual subscription. No performing rights fees.
I’d like to send you a free sample one slot, about 25 minutes. No cost, no obligation. If it fits the room, we can talk about a full day pack or a seasonal refresh arrangement.
Worth a listen?
[Name]
Three things working in this email:

• “No licensing fees” in the subject line leads with the pain point, not the product. Café owners think about costs constantly.

• “Four moods, one full operating day” signals that this isn’t a generic loop it’s a system built for how cafés actually operate.

• “Free sample — one slot” lowers the barrier to yes. They’re not buying anything. They’re pressing play on 25 minutes of music.
Step 4 : Pricing
Two models:

• One-time full pack: $120–200 for all 24 tracks. Clean transaction. Good for cafés that want a set-and-forget solution.

• Seasonal refresh: $280–400/year for four updates, a new pack each quarter reflecting seasonal mood shifts (bright spring mornings, cozy winter evenings). This is the recurring revenue model.
The seasonal pitch works specifically because café owners already think seasonally menu changes, décor updates, promotional periods. Music as a seasonal refresh fits naturally into how they already operate their business.

• Ten cafés on a $320/year seasonal model = $3,200 recurring. Low-maintenance once the production system is running.
Step 5 : Delivery and Rights
Deliver via a private Gumroad link or a Dropbox folder. Include a one-page document stating:

• Music is original and AI-generated

• Commercial use rights granted to the purchasing venue

• Permitted for in-premises playback without additional licensing obligations

• Resale or redistribution not permitted
Keep it simple. One page. Plain language. If you plan to scale past 10 clients, have a lawyer review it once, a one-time cost that’s covered by two sales.
Turning This Into a System
The prompt framework above is repeatable. Once you’ve built a morning slow jazz pack, you can rebuild it for a new city, a new season, or a different café aesthetic (jazz bar vs. specialty coffee vs. hotel café) by adjusting the era anchor and room description.
The production infrastructure how to organize tracks, manage client relationships, price your tiers, and track recurring revenue is what I built out in From Prompt to First Profit: Full Suno AI Money System 2026. The B2B channel has its own module including an outreach tracking template and a pricing calculator.
→ From Prompt to First Profit — Full Suno AI Money System 2026
If you’re still deciding which revenue channels are worth pursuing before building a full production system, the AI Music Monetization Map shows every channel : B2B licensing, streaming, digital products, sync with realistic effort and revenue ranges for each.
→ AI Music Monetization Map: How to License & Sell Your Music Safely
Part 3 of the Suno AI Sound Design Series covers lo-fi, the genre with the largest study and focus audience and the most direct path to passive streaming income. Follow to get notified.
